description: Apply a propranolol antidote rationale and answer-option revision to one existing live question record in place while preserving record ID, pool state, reviews, and learner history.

fields.citation: Cone DC, Brice JH, Delbridge TR, Myers JB, eds. Emergency Medical Services: Clinical Practice and Systems Oversight. 3rd ed. John Wiley & Sons; 2021:378.
fields.rationale: Propranolol is a beta blocker and can also produce sodium-channel blockade in overdose. In this scenario, however, the QRS is narrow, so the beta-blockade effects predominate; glucagon is therefore the best next antidote choice for bradycardia and hypotension after calcium has not improved perfusion. A would be more compelling if the electrocardiogram showed QRS widening from sodium-channel blockade. B is less appropriate because calcium has already failed to improve perfusion and the ingestion is a beta blocker rather than a calcium-channel blocker. C is a real antidote pairing, but fomepizole treats toxic alcohol ingestion, not this toxidrome.
fields.stem: A patient is bradycardic and hypotensive after ingesting a large amount of propranolol. The rhythm is narrow-complex, glucose is low-normal, and calcium has not improved perfusion. Which antidote pairing is most appropriate?
fields.title: Antidote Matching in Toxic Shock

updates.position_statement: Clarified propranolol beta-blocker toxicity treatment and narrowed answer-option wording
updates.reason: Single-question revision supplied 2026-07-05: clarify propranolol beta-blocker toxicity rationale while preserving sodium-channel blockade teaching detail.
